sections.title: 10. Simplification to ensure a future proof consumer protection framework.
path: 10. Simplification to ensure a future proof consumer protection framework.

paragraphs.text: eBay
eBay Response to the European Commission Consultation on A Digital Fairness Act

eBay welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission’s vision to further promote a fair, transparent, and trustworthy digital environment through a potential Digital Fairness Act (DFA). As a global online marketplace facilitating economic opportunity for millions of SMEs and consumers across the EU, we support a modern consumer policy that protects users from dangers while encouraging innovation, diversity of services, and responsible platform governance.

eBay strongly believes that both businesses and consumers, as well as the European Union as a whole, would benefit from a more streamlined legislative mindset. Legislative intervention should be clearly targeted based on a clear evidence basis and comprehensive risk analysis to avoid inconsistencies and an overall reduction of the compliance burden in line with the European Commission’s simplification agenda.

In the field of consumer protection, we believe the EU already possesses a strong and evolving legislative framework — including the UCPD, CPC Regulation, DSA, and GDPR. To be effective, new policy actions should not only build on existing instruments but also build on these existing regimes, focus on addressing clearly identified and specific harmful practices, and take into account the diversity of online service models — such as marketplaces, social platforms, content providers, and retail ecosystems. Any legislative action should also be preceded by consistent enforcement and a clear evaluation thereof.

We encourage the European Commission to view the Digital Fairness Act not only through the lens of potential new legislation but rather as the opportunity to explore a wider range of legislation options where needed, including clarificatory guidance among others.

A balanced, outcomes-focused approach can ensure that consumer trust is enhanced, SMEs continue to thrive, and innovation is not hindered by overly rigid requirements that do not reflect the reality of different digital environments.
Explore a wider range of existing legislation and potential legislative mechanisms where there is an identifiable need beyond solely new legislation.

Respect the existing consumer acquis and build on the principles-based approach — avoiding duplication and ensuring an evidence-based, proportionate approach whilst retaining flexibility and technology neutrality.
Strengthen enforcement tools and coordination — including risk-based approaches and cross-border collaboration.
Recognise the diversity of digital services — tailor obligations based on the role and control level of each actor.
Target persisting dark patterns effectively — focusing on manipulative outcomes, not specific design elements.
Support existing transparency measures in personalisation and fair marketing — without conflating relevance-based features with harm.
Recognise existing efforts surrounding influencer marketing — focusing on enforcement and awareness.
Work to harmonise pricing practices — ensuring that rules are understandable, easily implementable and avoiding cross-border fragmentation.
Ensure cancellation procedures are informed and proportional — avoiding both unnecessary friction and unintentional cancellations.
Promote simplification and legal coherence — especially for SMEs, circular business models, and second-hand goods, by reducing overlap and clarifying expectations.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: As a global online marketplace facilitating millions of transactions across the EU, eBay plays a vital role in empowering consumers, supporting SMEs, and advancing trust, safety, and innovation in digital commerce. In the EU, we provide vital services for small and micro businesses to access digital markets, contribute to the circular economy, and grow through export opportunities.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: Our role involves facilitating transactions, offering tools for trust and safety, and ensuring platform-level compliance with applicable regulations. We support the EU's objective to strengthen fairness and transparency in digital commerce and believe a tailored, evidence-based regulatory approach is key to achieving this without disrupting legitimate services that benefit SMEs and consumers alike.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: Trust is not a passive outcome - it is a deliberate commitment. At eBay, we have introduced a range of initiatives, protections, and programme features that are designed to ensure that anyone who uses our platform - whether buyer or seller - can engage with confidence and transparency.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: Examples of such initiatives include:

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: eBay Money Back Guarantee: Our core buyer protection programme, which ensures that consumers receive the item they ordered or get their money back - covering most transactions and eliminating complexity in disputes.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: Payment Holds: A consumer-focused safeguard that enables eBay to temporarily hold funds from a seller until order fulfilment or resolution of an issue, helping deter bad-faith selling and fraud.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: Authenticity Guarantee: An authentication programme for high-value categories like sneakers, watches, and luxury items - giving buyers peace of mind and protecting against counterfeit goods.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: "Car Fits" and Free Returns on Car Parts: Category-specific buyer protection tools that help consumers confirm compatibility before purchase and return eligible parts free of charge if needed - enhancing clarity in a complex category.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: eBay Local: A dedicated feature for local pickup transactions that increases user convenience and expands the accessibility of second-hand and bulky goods, supporting affordability and the circular economy.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: Proactive Buyer and Seller Education: Tailored messaging, prompts, and resolution guidance across the transaction journey to reduce disputes and build mutual accountability.

section_path: 1. eBay's approach to ensuring customer safety
paragraphs.text: These features reflect our broader commitment to a safe, fair, and user-centric digital commerce environment - one that can serve the needs of millions of individuals and businesses while aligning with the goals of EU consumer and digital policy.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The EU currently possesses one of the strongest and most comprehensive consumer protection frameworks worldwide. "EU citizens therefore enjoy some of the strongest consumer rights in the world." The EU has consistently updated and renewed its rules in light of new potential challenges and risks, including with the introduction of new pieces of legislation and consumer protection mechanisms. In this context, businesses have diligently worked to ensure their compliance with these new rules. It is therefore important to recognise their efforts and the fact that any new piece of legislation carries a substantial burden, in particular for SMEs, that need to adapt, evaluate, study their procedures and implement any new rules.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: Key instruments include:

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD);

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The Consumer Rights Directive;

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The Price Indication Directive (PID);

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The Omnibus Directive;

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The Digital Services Act (DSA);

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The AI Act;

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The General Data Protection Regulation;

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: The Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: These rules already serve to address many of the concerns outlined in the Digital Fairness Act consultation.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: With this in mind and in the context of the EU's renewed simplification drive, it is vital that new pieces of legislation do not become the first point of call when it comes to addressing any new or potential challenges. The EU has at its disposal a wider range of potential mechanisms to tackle these challenges, including enforcement of existing legislation, clarificatory guidance or targeted amendments.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: As a prerequisite to new legislation and to avoid potential overlap or compliance confusion, we strongly urge the Commission to:

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: Take full stock of existing consumer law mechanisms both legislative and non;

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: Ensure new rules are evidence-based, proportionate, and precisely scoped;

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: Conduct a full impact assessment of the consumer law obligations introduced since 2022, including newer consumer protection mechanisms in pieces of legislation such as the Digital Services Act, AI Act and more.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: Explore a wider range of potential actions to tackle new challenges not limited to legislative action but including clarificatory guidance, etc.

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: 1 https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/topics/consumer-protection/

section_path: 2. Explore a Wider Range of Legislative Options & Respect the Existing Acquis
paragraphs.text: This approach is crucial to avoid legal fragmentation, ensure clarity for consumers, and preserve the capacity of SMEs and platforms to comply while continuing to innovate. Bearing in mind the high level objective of the European Commission to simplify legislation and to its better regulation principles, it is essential that any new legislation complements existing frameworks to avoid contradictory and overlapping rules which will further exacerbate implementation efforts and create uncertainty, in particular for SMEs.
The EU's consumer protection framework has long been anchored in a principles-based approach, particularly through instruments such as the UCPD. This model has proven to be:
Technology-neutral, allowing rules to adapt to evolving digital practices;
Future-proof, accommodating emerging business models and user interfaces;
Flexible, enabling enforcement authorities to assess context, intent, and outcomes on a case-by-case basis.

Together with the UCPD blacklist, the existing framework provides strong overall protection by combining principles-based rules with prescriptive prohibitions where evidence outlines a specific and targeted issue.
We thus encourage the European Commission to:
Maintain and reinforce the principles-based nature of the existing rules as the default, only introducing prescriptive bans where there is a clear, identifiable, and demonstrable consumer harm.

There is a recognised gap in the enforcement of consumer legislation in the European Union, often due to various challenges such as lack of resources or fragmentation at the national level as well as the differing priorities for enforcement authorities nationally. In the short-term, it is important that ongoing concerns and challenges are tackled adequately whilst in the long-term a more holistic approach to enforcement is needed to ensure that both existing and future rules are put into practice. The practical suggestions below aim to take both these contexts into account and if implemented may serve to tackle a lot of the concerns outlined in the DFA consultation.

The current lack of resources is widespread and thus requires a prioritization of enforcement authority resources. We thus encourage the European Commission and enforcement authorities to consider the following:
A risk-based enforcement would allow authorities to distinguish between systemic abuse and good-faith actors, especially SMEs and individual sellers.
In the long-term, a more outcome-based regulatory environment would further support enforcement authorities rather than overly prescriptive
obligations ensuring that legislation is future-proof and easily adaptable to new challenges and concerns and ensuring more legal certainty.

We support the ongoing work by both the European Commission and national enforcement authorities towards the development and use of **technology-assisted enforcement tools** to help prioritize, detect patterns of non-compliance, and use resources efficiently. We encourage authorities with existing tools to share such best practices widely to encourage their uptake and development. Existing examples already demonstrate a willingness to further encourage a more technologically centered culture of enforcement such as the ARBUZ tool developed by UOKiK, the Polish consumer protection authority AI tool which assists them in detecting abusive contract clauses.

We encourage the **European Commission to explore EU wide technological enforcement solutions** similarly to those being introduced in the digital identity space, for example. These solutions should be technology and business neutral and could be funded by existing mechanisms such as the Technical Support Instrument (TSI).
Collaborative guidance development between regulators and industry, especially for rapidly evolving areas such as dark patterns, personalisation, or influencer marketing.

Timely and targeted guidance is also key. We have repeatedly witnessed a delay in the development of clarificatory guidance leaving stakeholders at odds with how to implement the measures if guidance is even provided before the implementation deadlines. We encourage the Commission to strengthen its better policymaking principles to ensure that enough implementation time is allowed to firstly develop clear guidance and secondly allow actors enough time to actually implement that guidance.

We support the European Commission's plans to review the CPC Regulation as a means to further strengthen consumers in the EU single market. As part of the review, we encourage the European Commission to consider the following aspects:

We appreciate the European Commission considering the wide industry call for stronger enforcement. We thus support the Commission evaluating the **feasibility of introducing centralized enforcement** at the EU level for certain very specific cases in consumer policy, for example in high-impact cross-border cases.

In this context and in line with the European Commission's recent work on enhancing dialogue between stakeholders such as through implementation dialogues as well as the practices in other policy fields such as antitrust, we encourage the Commission to evaluate the possibility of including a specific framework for dialogue and exchanges with all societal stakeholders during case procedures by the CPC and/or European Commission. This is especially important due to the speed and changeability of the online space and services and could be the most practical way to address concerns quickly and efficiently.
The review of the CPC should also be an opportunity to further explore how to ensure a more consistent enforcement across Member States to avoid
instances of regulatory fragmentation, for example, through the introduction of stronger collaborative mechanisms throughout the CPC.

Improved enforcement — rather than new overlapping legislation — can address many of the concerns highlighted in this consultation more efficiently and without overburdening platforms that do not control the entire consumer journey.

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: Digital services differ significantly in how they operate, what they control, and the types of risks they may pose. Regulation should take these differences into account and avoid applying identical obligations across fundamentally different service models.

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: That is why it is crucial that any definitions introduced as part of any new Act are targeted and proportionate, ensuring that service offerings not presenting identified challenges are not inadvertently included. For example, it is vital that any new definitions on the elements below are targeted to clearly identified and substantiated challenges linked to specific services. This is particularly the case for definitions such as addictive design, lootboxes or problematic advertising which can vary widely depending on the service and if not defined well may inadvertently encompass features that do not possess the same risk profile.

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: A one-size-fits-all approach risks:

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: Over-regulating certain actors while under-regulating high-risk models in a way that ultimately does not benefit consumers;

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: Confusing enforcement standards, especially when cross-cutting rules are applied inconsistently across sectors.

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: We therefore encourage the Commission to design any new obligations or guidance with the principle of proportionality and outcome in mind. Actors should be assessed based on:

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: Their actual level of control over user-facing content and decisions;

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: Their role in shaping the commercial strategy (e.g. price, promotion, marketing);

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: The risks inherent in their business model and the nature of their relationship with the consumer;

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: How effective the tailored and proportionate solutions provided are in achieving the goals set out by regulators.

section_path: 5. Service Diversity and Targeted Proportionate Rules
paragraphs.text: This approach will help ensure rules are targeted, fair, and enforceable across the variety of services that make up today's digital economy.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: eBay supports the prohibition of deceptive interface designs that distort or manipulate consumer choice. Regulation in this area must be clear, proportionate, and grounded in actual consumer harm.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Importantly, the European Union already has a robust legal framework to address most forms of dark patterns. The UCPD provides a horizontal instrument to prohibit misleading

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: and aggressive commercial practices, and has been used successfully in multiple Member States to tackle deceptive design. In addition, both the GDPR and the Digital Services Act (DSA) contain provisions that target manipulation, misleading user interfaces, and deceptive choice architectures.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Recent studies confirm this view, highlighting that many problematic practices can already be addressed under existing law. In fact, these views recognise that most Member State authorities agree that the UCPD can cover a broad range of dark patterns when applied in combination with the Directive's general clauses and blacklist items. The main challenge lies in enforcement consistency and in providing clear, contextual guidance.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: It is also important to recognise that case law is emerging at both national and EU levels to further define and refine these boundaries, showcasing the importance and flexibility of the existing principles based approach. Recent CPC-led actions, and competition authority interventions in online interfaces, provide useful precedents that help establish what constitutes misleading urgency, non-transparent consent, or manipulative default settings. The recent Amazon case in Poland, for example, also highlights the ability of the existing framework to evolve and be used to tackle new challenges.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: In light of this, we support the Commission's intent to address persisting dark patterns, but we recommend:

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Avoiding duplicative legislation or definitions that might create fragmentation or regulatory confusion;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Aligning the different definitions of dark patterns through clarificatory guidance and aligned with the most recent CPC and OECD work;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Focusing on enforcement consistency and targeted soft-law instruments, such as guidance and examples for enforcement and compliance;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: However, where there is a clear, evidence-based need for additional clarity, we support a targeted amendment to the UCPD blacklist to explicitly prohibit the most harmful and well-documented dark patterns. Such an approach must:

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Be narrowly focused on patterns with demonstrated consumer harm;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Avoid penalising user-centric design practices or legitimate commercial differentiation;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Preserve the UCPD's strengths as a flexible and outcome-based instrument.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Personalisation plays a positive role in helping consumers find what they need more efficiently — particularly in choice-rich environments like online marketplaces. When transparency is ensured and personalisation is based on user-relevant data, it improves trust and decision-making.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: ²https://ecommerce-europe.eu/press-item/study-on-digital-fairness-enforcement-of-eu-law-key-to-protecting-consumers-shopping-online/ ³https://commission.europa.eu/live-work-travel-eu/consumer-rights-and-complaints/enforcement-consumer-protection/consumer-protection-cooperation-network_en ⁴https://uokik.gov.pl/en/pln-31-million-fine-for-amazon

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: At eBay, personalisation enables buyers to discover relevant listings, receive alerts about price changes, and identify sustainable choices like second-hand goods/refurbished goods — supporting both convenience and circular economy goals.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: However, it's essential to distinguish between:

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Fair personalisation: designed to enhance discoverability, relevance, and user satisfaction, based on observable or declared preferences;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Manipulative personalisation: designed to steer behaviour in ways that undermine autonomy — e.g. by using hidden profiling, psychological triggers, or opaque recommendation logic.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Legislation in this space should therefore remain outcome-based, not prescriptive. The focus should be on whether the result is misleading or exploitative, rather than whether a particular interface element or algorithm is used. Regulating design itself would risk restricting innovation and legitimate user experience enhancements.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: We support clearer enforcement under existing frameworks such as the UCPD and GDPR, and better guidance to identify harmful personalisation. But we caution against broad new restrictions that could conflate useful relevance-based tools with unfair practices.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Specifically on influencer marketing, there is already a broad set of applicable EU legislative and non-legislative rules that prohibit covert or misleading commercial practices. These include the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the ePrivacy Directive, and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD), which explicitly addresses commercial content disseminated through video-sharing platforms — including influencer advertising, as well as the Consumer Fairness Pledge

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: eBay welcomed and signed the European Commission's Consumer Protection Pledge including measures to promote transparent influencer marketing.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: The key challenge is therefore not the lack of legal basis, but rather the complexity of enforcement, especially across borders and fast-evolving digital channels.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: We recommend:

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Continued implementation of the Commission's Consumer Protection Pledge and existing legislation;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Prioritising enforcement against undisclosed commercial content;

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: Avoiding regulatory spillover onto platforms that do not initiate or control influencer campaigns. There is already a broad set of applicable EU rules that prohibit covert or misleading commercial practices.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: A proportionate, risk-based approach will best support consumer protection while enabling responsible marketing practices across digital ecosystems.

section_path: 6. Clarifying Dark Patterns and Existing Provisions
paragraphs.text: At the same time, we recognise the potential dangers of the widespread marketing of potentially harmful content such as those relating to body image for example. In this context, we support the European Commission examining potential new measures, however, would suggest that this be done within the context of the future AVMSD review.

Pricing transparency is vital for consumer trust, however, before exploring new rules surrounding pricing, it is important to note that the current EU regulatory landscape — particularly the implementation of the Price Indication Directive (PID) and Omnibus Directive — has introduced significant confusion for businesses, especially SMEs and cross-border sellers and would benefit from harmonising guidance or leadership from the EU.
While the goal of transparency is sound, the practical application of these rules has already led to:
Inconsistent interpretations across Member States, especially concerning the requirement to display the "lowest price in the last 30 days" before a promotion;
Difficulty in understanding and applying PID rules, especially for multi-seller platforms and dynamic pricing environments;
Operational and legal uncertainty, which discourages sellers from engaging in EU-wide campaigns and limits promotional flexibility.

These problems are particularly pronounced for marketplaces like eBay, where sellers operate across jurisdictions and apply their own pricing strategies. Sellers are often unsure how to comply with national requirements while maintaining a consistent consumer experience.
Rather than layering on new obligations, this should be seen as an opportunity for simplification. Specifically, we recommend:
Clarifying and harmonising the interpretation of the 30-day lowest price rule, with specific consideration for:
Second-hand and refurbished goods
Collectibles or unique-condition items;
Personalised products;
Fast-moving tech products with high pricing volatility;
Preserving the ability to use RRPs in a transparent, responsible way, with clear criteria to avoid misuse;
Supporting cross-border consistency, by issuing EU-level guidance that reduces divergence and enables compliance in multi-country campaigns;
Avoiding new or more rigid pricing rules that could further discourage price competition, transparency, or SME participation.

eBay supports simple and transparent cancellation processes that empower consumers. However, recent proposals to introduce a universal 'one-click cancellation' standard may have unintended consequences.
For marketplaces like eBay, cancellations:
Often involve third-party sellers with their own fulfilment systems;
May trigger automated logistics or financial processes (e.g. shipping, invoicing, refunds);
May result in unintended loss of features by consumers;
Can affect seller trust and satisfaction if abused or accidental.
While cancellations must be straightforward, they should also:
Include clear confirmation steps that inform consumers what they are cancelling;
Prevent accidental cancellations that may disadvantage both buyer and seller;
Allow platforms to tailor cancellation flows to match their role and technical structure.
We recommend:
Upholding the Consumer Rights Directive's requirement that cancellation is not unduly burdensome;
Avoiding one-size-fits-all mandates like one-click cancellation unless clearly justified;
Encouraging contextual, proportionate user flows that reflect service diversity and preserve consumer intention.

section_path: 10. Simplification to ensure a future proof consumer protection framework.
paragraphs.text: As eBay, we very much welcome the European Commission's focus on simplification as part of its consultation on a Digital Fairness Package. In this context, we would like to outline two key recommendations"

section_path: 10. Simplification to ensure a future proof consumer protection framework.
paragraphs.text: Consumer information requirements should be aligned with the Digital Age. Where possible and feasible, information requirements should be digitised and integrated into existing and upcoming initiatives such as the Digital Product Passport. This would allow consumers to more easily access the information they need whilst avoiding potential consumer information overload.

section_path: 10. Simplification to ensure a future proof consumer protection framework.
paragraphs.text: More widely, a more targeted consumer study should be undertaken to gather a clear evidence basis for those pieces of information consumers actually benefit from and those that only serve to further overload consumers in their everyday lives. This

section_path: 10. Simplification to ensure a future proof consumer protection framework.
paragraphs.text: would aid policymakers in targeting any potential future information requirements as well as strengthen the simplification exercise currently being carried out.

eBay has been and remains committed to supporting the European Union's vision for a fair, transparent, and responsible digital economy. We strongly believe this can best be achieved through a balanced and evidence-based approach that makes full use of the EU's already advanced consumer protection framework.

We encourage the Commission to use the Digital Fairness Act as an opportunity to strengthen enforcement, coherence, and clarity — and to address specific, well-identified challenges through the most appropriate mechanism. That may not always require new legislation. In many cases, targeted guidance, coordinated enforcement, or a clarification of existing rules can be more effective and less burdensome for consumers, SMEs, and platforms alike.
Key priorities should include:
Building on the consumer acquis, not duplicating it;
Prioritising proportionate, outcomes-based interventions rather than prescriptive obligations;
Tailoring rules to reflect service diversity — recognising the different roles, responsibilities, and risk profiles of intermediaries, retailers, and content-based platforms;
Ensuring legal simplification and coherence, particularly for cross-border sellers and SMEs who face disproportionate regulatory complexity.

We stand ready to continue working with the European Commission and enforcement authorities — sharing the perspective of a neutral, seller-driven marketplace committed to enabling trusted digital commerce, empowering consumers, and advancing sustainable economic opportunity across Europe.
